attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching.
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks to use the Claude API, Anthropic SDKs, or Managed Agents (`/v1/agents`, `/v1/sessions`); user asks to add, modify, debug, optimize, or improve a Claude feature (prompt caching, cache hit rate, adaptive thinking, compaction, code_execution, batch, files API, citations, memory tool) or a Claude model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; or user asks about prompt caching / cache hit rate / cache reads / cache creation in any project that uses the Anthropic SDK (even without mentioning Claude by name).
DO NOT TRIGGER when: file imports `openai`/non-Anthropic SDK, filename signals another provider (`agent-openai.py`, `*-generic.py`), code is provider-neutral, or task is general programming/ML.

1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### Schema example
237	
238	Stdin:
239	
240	```json
241	{
242	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
243	  "steps": [
244	    {
245	      "op": "quote",
246	      "items": [
247	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
248	      ]
249	    },
250	    {
251	      "op": "claim",
252	      "policy": 0,
253	      "incident": {
254	        "cause": "fire",
255	        "damages": [
256	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
257	        ]
258	      }
259	    }
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	Stdout shape:
265	
266	```json
267	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
268	```
269	
270	## Expected Output Files
271	
272	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
273	- Test files
274	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
275	
276	## Constraints
277	
278	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
279	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
280	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
281	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
282

file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

Let me launch the test-list agent to create the comprehensive test list based on the prompt specification.

input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - Policy management system with quote and claim operations

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
CLI entry point: src/cli.ts

Requirements (from the Example Mapping in prompt.md):

The system supports two operations: **quote** (computes premium) and **claim** (processes damage report).

### Item values and base premiums:
- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Components (runes, moonstones): 250 G each, 25 G base premium per component
- Building block of 3 alike components: special base premium of 60 G

### Premium modifiers:
- Cursed items: +50% risk surcharge (on affected item only)
- High enchantment (≥5): +30% surcharge (on affected item only)
- Long-standing customer (≥2 years): -20% loyalty discount (policy-wide)
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge (policy-wide; every item in a quote is treated as first insurance)
- Follow-up contract (each contract after first): -15% discount (policy-wide)
- 5 G processing fee added at the very end

### Rounding:
- Premiums rounded up (in MHPCO's favor)
- Payouts rounded down (in MHPCO's favor)
- Intermediate amounts kept as fractions, only final result rounded

### Claim processing:
- 100 G deductible per damaged item
- Total payout per policy capped at 2× insurance sum
- Enchantment ≥8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount
- Dragon material: fully reimbursed
- Both clauses apply: 50% rule wins (then deductible)

### Examples from spec (use these for test cases):

**Building block of 3 alike components:**
- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

**"Alike" components:**
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

**Modifier scope on multi-item policies:**
- Policy with cursed sword (100 G) and plain amulet (60 G) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee (curse surcharge applies only to cursed item)

**Modifier thresholds:**
- Customer with exactly 2 years → loyalty discount applies
- Sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment surcharge applies
- Sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge

**Deductible:**
- Dragon attack damages sword (500 G) and amulet (300 G) → payout 600 G (100 G deductible per item)

**Standard reimbursement:**
- Regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
- Damage to rune, damage 200 G → payout 100 G

**Enchantment vs dragon material:**
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G (50% wins, then deductible)
- Dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G (dragon only, full reimburse minus deductible)
- Steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G (50% then deductible)

**Multiple items same type:**
- Policy with two swords → insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G
- Damages array with more entries than policy covers → error (non-zero exit)

**Cap exhaustion:**
- Policy with sword and amulet → insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G
- Cursed sword insurance value 1000 G → cap 2000 G (based on unmodified insurance value)
- Sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750 G (block doesn't affect insurance sum)
- Two successive claims of 1500 G on sword (cap 2000 G): first → 1400 G payout (600 G remaining), second → 600 G payout (0 remaining)

**Integration - Newcomer with cursed sword:**
- 0 years, no previous contract, cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3) → premium 165 G
- (100 base + 50 curse + 10 first insurance = 160 + 5 fee = 165)

**Integration - Long-standing customer's second contract:**
- 3 years, second quote, cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7) → premium 160 G
- (100 base + 50 curse + 30 high enchantment − 20 loyalty + 10 first insurance − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160)

**Edge cases:**
- Empty item list → premium 5 G (only processing fee)
- Unknown item type → non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Claim for item not in policy → non-zero exit, error to stderr
- Negative damage amount → non-zero exit, error to stderr

**Dragon-material sword, enchantment 8, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G** (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

content.text:
Let me verify there is no over-implementation:

- **Did I implement features for future tests?** No. The loop only handles "sword" because that is the only item type tested. Future tests for amulets, staffs, potions, etc. will force adding those base premiums.
- **Did I add logic not demanded by current tests?** No. The formula `base + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE` handles both cases: empty items (base=0, so premium=5) and a single sword (base=100, firstInsurance=10, premium=115).
- **Did I optimize prematurely?** No.
- **Did I refactor existing code?** No, this is a fresh implementation replacing the hardcoded return.

One thing I want to verify: the loop structure is justified because the test does pass an items array (even if it has only one element). Iterating over items is the natural minimal way to compute the base from the items array. The only item type handled is "sword" -- other types contribute 0, which is correct for now since no other types are tested.

Green Phase Complete:

**Implementation**: Added logic to compute premium from items in the scenario. For each item of type "sword", adds 100 to the base. Then applies 10% first insurance surcharge on the base, and adds the 5 G processing fee. Empty items yield base=0, so premium = 0 + 0 + 5 = 5. A single sword yields base=100, so premium = 100 + 10 + 5 = 115.

**Result**: All tests now pass (2 passing, 39 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal because only the "sword" item type is recognized (other types default to 0 base), and only the first step is processed. The formula directly follows from the two test cases without anticipating future requirements like loyalty discounts, cursed surcharges, or claim processing.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

- Code more clearly reveals intent -- the map shows "these are the base premiums by item type" in one glance

- Variable naming better describes accumulated total

content.text:
### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass**:

```
function calculateQuotePremium(items: any[]): number {
  const totalBasePremium = items.reduce(
    (sum: number, item: any) => sum + basePremiumFor(item),
    0,
  );
  const firstInsurance = totalBasePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
  return Math.ceil(totalBasePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE);
}

export function processScenario(scenario: any): unknown {
  const results = scenario.steps.map((step: any) => ({
    premium: calculateQuotePremium(step.items),
  }));
  return { results };
}
```

- Constants: 5, 0.1, "sword", 100, "amulet", 60, "staff", 80, "potion", 40, "rune", 25, 0 (nullish fallback), 0 (reduce initial) = 14 constants => 14

- Naming: All existing names remain appropriate. No renaming needed.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass**:

The code structure is the same logic but now distributed across three functions. The overall element count changes slightly:

**New elements introduced**:
- 2 new function bindings: `countByType`, `calculateBasePremium` 
- 2 new invocations: calling `countByType(items)` and `calculateBasePremium(typeCounts)`
- 2 new bindings: `counts` (renamed from `typeCounts`), `typeCounts` (parameter in `calculateBasePremium`)
- 1 new binding: `basePremium` (renamed from `totalBasePremium`)

**Elements removed**:
- The original `typeCounts` local variable (replaced by return value from `countByType`)
- The original `totalBasePremium` let variable (replaced by `basePremium` const)

Net change: mass increases slightly due to 2 additional invocations (+4) and a couple of additional bindings (+2). But 1 assignment is removed (the `let totalBasePremium = 0` is still there inside `calculateBasePremium`, so no net reduction in assignments).

**Approximate New Total Mass**: ~147 (up from ~141)

**Mass Change**: 141 -> 147 (delta: +6)

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names reviewed -- existing names adequate. New functions named `countByType` and `calculateBasePremium` clearly reveal intent.
- Structure: Extracted `countByType` and `calculateBasePremium` as separate helper functions from `calculateQuotePremium`.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Improved. `calculateQuotePremium` now reads as a clear three-step pipeline: count items by type, calculate base premium, apply surcharges and fee. Each step is named and self-documenting.
- Rule 2 trumps APP: Mass increased by ~6, but clarity improved significantly. The `calculateQuotePremium` function now reads as a concise summary of the premium calculation algorithm.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): `countByType` will be reusable when claim processing is added (claims need to know item types in the policy).
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Slight increase in elements, justified by improved clarity.

**Benefits**:
- `calculateQuotePremium` is now a concise 4-line function that reads like a specification
- Block discount logic is isolated in `calculateBasePremium`, making it easier to modify as more block discount tests are added
- `countByType` is a reusable utility for future claim processing
- Each function has a single clear responsibility

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `countByType` and `calculateBasePremium` helper functions from `calculateQuotePremium` to improve clarity and separation of concerns
- **Mass Change**: ~141 -> ~147 (+6, justified by Rule 2 improvement)
- **Tests**: All 9 passing

```json
}
```
Expected: `{"results": [{"premium": 165}]}`

The cursed surcharge adds 50% to the cursed item's base premium (item-specific modifier). 100 base + 50 curse = 150. Then policy-wide: 150 * 10% first insurance = 15. But wait - the spec says first insurance is 10% of the policy base premium (sum of item base premiums), not including item surcharges. Let me re-read...

Actually from the spec: "item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base premium (the sum of all item base premiums)"

So policy base premium = 100 (sword base). First insurance = 10% of 100 = 10.
Item surcharge: curse = 50% of 100 = 50.
Total = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160 + 5 fee = 165.

Current code: no curse handling. It will compute: base=100, firstInsurance=10, premium=ceil(100+10+5)=115.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The refactored `calculateItemSurcharges`:

**Before** (mutable loop version):
- Bindings: `items` (param), `total`, `item` = 3 x 1 = 3
- Constants: `0` (init), `0` (fallback) = 2 x 1 = 2
- Invocations: `BASE_PREMIUM[]` = 1 x 2 = 2
- Conditionals: `if (item.cursed)`, `??` = 2 x 4 = 8
- Loops: `for...of` = 1 x 5 = 5
- Assignments: `let total = 0`, `total +=` = 2 x 6 = 12
- **Subtotal: 32**

**After** (filter/reduce version):
- Bindings: `items` (param), `item` (filter callback), `total` (reduce), `item` (reduce) = 4 x 1 = 4
- Constants: `0` (reduce init), `0` (fallback) = 2 x 1 = 2
- Invocations: `filter()`, `reduce()`, `BASE_PREMIUM[]` = 3 x 2 = 6
- Conditionals: `??` = 1 x 4 = 4
- Loops: `filter` (iteration), `reduce` (iteration) = 2 x 5 = 10
- Assignments: 0 x 6 = 0
- **Subtotal: 26**

**Mass Change for `calculateItemSurcharges`**: 32 --> 26 (delta -6)

**Overall Mass Change**: 211 --> 205 (delta -6)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names reviewed and found adequate. No renaming needed.
- Structural change: Replaced mutable accumulation pattern (`let total = 0; for...if...total += ...`) with declarative `filter().reduce()` pipeline in `calculateItemSurcharges`.
- Mass: Reduced from 211 to 205 (delta -6), primarily by eliminating 2 assignments (the mutable `let total = 0` initialization and the `total +=` mutation).
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The `filter().reduce()` pipeline more clearly communicates the two-step logic: first select cursed items, then sum their surcharges. The imperative version interleaves selection and accumulation.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Eliminated the mutable `total` variable entirely.

**Trade-off**: The functional version introduces an extra iteration (filter + reduce vs single loop), but at the current scale this is negligible, and the clarity improvement justifies it. The conditional check (`if (item.cursed)`) was absorbed into the declarative `filter`, making the code's intent more transparent.

Note: I did not refactor `calculateBasePremium` the same way because its conditional logic (block pricing vs per-item pricing) is more complex and the imperative style with `if/else` is clearer for that case.

## Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Replaced mutable loop in `calculateItemSurcharges` with declarative `filter().reduce()` pipeline
- **Mass Change**: 211 --> 205 (delta -6)
- **Tests**: All 14 passing

input.prompt:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "should apply 30% surcharge for sword with enchantment exactly 5 (0-year customer) — 100 base + 30 enchantment + 10 first insurance = 140 + 5 fee = 145"
Expected behavior: Items with enchantment >= 5 get a 30% surcharge on their base premium (item-specific modifier)
Current error: Expected 145, received 115
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Current calculateItemSurcharges only handles cursed. Add enchantment >= 5 check.

The function currently:
```typescript
function calculateItemSurcharges(items: any[]): number {
  return items
    .filter((item: any) => item.cursed)
    .reduce((total: number, item: any) => total + (BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0) * CURSED_SURCHARGE_RATE, 0);
}
```

This only handles cursed. We need to also check enchantment >= 5 for each item. The surcharges are additive per item - each item can have both cursed AND enchantment surcharges.

Minimal approach: change calculateItemSurcharges to iterate over all items and sum both surcharges where applicable.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:

Introduced two constants (`ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`) and rewrote the function to iterate over all items, applying the cursed surcharge when `item.cursed` is true and the enchantment surcharge when `item.enchantment >= 5`. Both surcharges are additive per item.

**Result**: All tests now pass (15 passing, 26 todo).

**Approach**: This is minimal because it only adds what the current test demands -- a 30% surcharge on base premium for items with enchantment >= 5. The function was restructured from a filter/reduce chain to a simple loop so it can check both conditions per item, which is the simplest way to handle the two independent surcharge conditions. No future features were added.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored Code Mass**:

**Constants** (Mass: 1 each): Same 22 constants + 1 new constant (`0` in `getItemBasePremium`'s `?? 0`) = 23. But wait -- the `0` in `calculateItemSurcharges` for `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` was replaced by the call to `getItemBasePremium`, so that `0` was removed. Net: still 22 constants.

Actually let me recount more carefully:

Before refactoring, `calculateItemSurcharges` had `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` which contained one constant `0`. After refactoring, that line became `getItemBasePremium(item)` -- no constant. But the new function `getItemBasePremium` has `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` which has one constant `0`. So the constant count stays at 22.

**Bindings** (Mass: 1 each): Added `item` parameter in `getItemBasePremium` = 1 new binding. Total: 29.

**Invocations** (Mass: 2 each): 
- Removed: `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` read and `??` in calculateItemSurcharges = -2 invocations
- Added: `getItemBasePremium(item)` call in calculateItemSurcharges = +1 invocation
- Added: `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` read and `??` in getItemBasePremium = +2 invocations
- Net: +1 invocation. Total: 17 x 2 = 34.

**Conditionals**: Unchanged. 3 x 4 = 12.

**Loops**: Unchanged. 4 x 5 = 20.

**Assignments**: Unchanged. 5 x 6 = 30.

**Total Mass: 22 + 29 + 34 + 12 + 20 + 30 = 147**

Mass Change: 144 -> 147 (delta +3)

The mass increased slightly, which is expected when extracting a named function. However, this refactoring is justified by Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) -- the `getItemBasePremium` function:
1. Encapsulates the "look up an item's base premium" concept into a named function
2. Will be reusable as more code needs to look up individual item base premiums (e.g., future claim calculations will need insurance values based on base premiums)
3. Makes `calculateItemSurcharges` slightly more readable by replacing `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` with a semantically meaningful call

Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass, so the slight mass increase is acceptable.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names already clearly reveal intent. No renaming needed.
- Extracted `getItemBasePremium(item)` helper function (Rule 2 -- Reveals Intent, Rule 3 -- prepares for reuse in claim calculations)
- Mass: Increased from 144 to 147 (delta +3) -- acceptable trade-off for improved intent revelation
- Rule 2 justification: The helper function names the concept of "looking up an item's base premium" which makes `calculateItemSurcharges` read more naturally

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactoring**: Extracted `getItemBasePremium(item)` helper function to encapsulate base premium lookup. The helper will also be reusable as more code needs individual item base premium lookups.

**Mass Change**: 144 -> 147 (delta +3, justified by Rule 2 trumping APP)

All 23 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Let me calculate the mass of the refactored `processScenario`:

```typescript
export function processScenario(scenario: any): unknown {
  const policies: any[] = [];                    // binding(policies), constant([])
  const results: any[] = [];                     // binding(results), constant([])
  let quoteIndex = 0;                            // binding(quoteIndex), assignment(=0)

  for (const step of scenario.steps) {           // loop, binding(step), binding(scenario)
    if (step.op === "claim") {                   // conditional, constant("claim")
      const policy = policies[step.policy];      // binding(policy)
      results.push(calculateClaimResult(policy, step.incident));
                                                 // invocation(push), invocation(calculateClaimResult)
    } else {
      policies.push({ items: step.items });      // invocation(push)
      results.push({                             // invocation(push)
        premium: calculateQuotePremium(step.items, scenario.customer, quoteIndex),
                                                 // invocation(calculateQuotePremium)
      });
      quoteIndex++;                              // assignment(++)
    }
  }
  return { results };
}
```

**processScenario mass**:
- Constants: `[]`, `[]`, `"claim"` = 3 => 3
- Bindings: `policies`, `results`, `quoteIndex`, `step`, `scenario`, `policy` = 6 => 6
- Invocations: `push` (claim), `calculateClaimResult`, `push` (quote policies), `push` (quote results), `calculateQuotePremium` = 5 => 10
- Conditionals: 1 => 4
- Loops: 1 => 5
- Assignments: `quoteIndex = 0`, `quoteIndex++` = 2 => 12

**processScenario mass: 3 + 6 + 10 + 4 + 5 + 12 = 40** (was 79)

**calculateClaimResult mass**:
```typescript
function calculateClaimResult(policy: any, incident: any): { ... } {
  const insuranceSum = calculateInsuranceSum(policy.items);  // binding, invocation
  const cap = insuranceSum * CAP_MULTIPLIER;                 // binding
  let totalPayout = 0;                                       // binding, assignment
  for (const damage of incident.damages) {                   // loop, binding
    totalPayout += damage.amount - DEDUCTIBLE;               // assignment
  }
  totalPayout = Math.floor(totalPayout);                     // assignment, invocation
  const remainingCap = cap - totalPayout;                    // binding
  return { payout: totalPayout, remainingCap };
}
```

- Constants: `0` = 1 => 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `insuranceSum`, `cap`, `totalPayout`, `damage`, `remainingCap` = 7 => 7
- Invocations: `calculateInsuranceSum`, `Math.floor` = 2 => 4
- Conditionals: 0 => 0
- Loops: 1 => 5
- Assignments: `totalPayout = 0`, `totalPayout +=`, `totalPayout =` = 3 => 18

**calculateClaimResult mass: 1 + 7 + 4 + 0 + 5 + 18 = 35**

**calculateInsuranceSum mass**:
```typescript
function calculateInsuranceSum(items: any[]): number {
  return items.reduce(
    (sum: number, item: any) => sum + (INSURANCE_VALUE[item.type] ?? 0),
    0
  );
}
```

- Constants: `0` (fallback), `0` (init) = 2 => 2
- Bindings: `items`, `sum`, `item` = 3 => 3
- Invocations: `reduce`, `+`, `INSURANCE_VALUE[]` = 3 => 6
- Conditionals: 0 => 0
- Loops: 1 (reduce) => 5
- Assignments: 0 => 0

**calculateInsuranceSum mass: 2 + 3 + 6 + 0 + 5 + 0 = 16**

**Combined new mass: 40 + 35 + 16 = 91** vs. original 79 for processScenario alone.

The total mass increased slightly because the extraction added function signatures (new bindings) and an extra invocation. However, this is a Rule 2 improvement -- clarity trumps APP mass. The `processScenario` function is now significantly easier to read (mass 40 vs 79), and each function has a single clear responsibility.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: All names already clearly reveal intent. No changes needed.
- Structure (Rule 2): Extracted `calculateClaimResult` and `calculateInsuranceSum` functions from inline claim processing in `processScenario`. This mirrors the pattern already established with `calculateQuotePremium` for quote processing.
- Also extracted the magic number `2` (cap multiplier) into a named constant `CAP_MULTIPLIER`.

Benefits:
- `processScenario` is now a clean dispatcher that delegates to `calculateQuotePremium` for quotes and `calculateClaimResult` for claims -- symmetric and easy to follow.
- `calculateInsuranceSum` is extracted as a reusable building block that will be needed as claim logic grows.
- `processScenario` mass reduced from 79 to 40 (the complexity was moved into focused, well-named functions).
- Magic number `2` replaced with named constant `CAP_MULTIPLIER`.

Trade-off: Total combined mass increased from 79 to 91, but this is acceptable because Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) takes priority over APP mass.

## Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `calculateClaimResult` and `calculateInsuranceSum` functions; added `CAP_MULTIPLIER` constant.
- **Mass Change**: `processScenario` 79 -> 40 (combined new total 91, acceptable trade-off for clarity)
- **Tests**: All 23 passing

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Let me calculate the mass of the refactored code.

**New `calculateDamageReimbursement` function:**
```
function calculateDamageReimbursement(damage: any, policyItems: any[]): number {
  const policyItem = policyItems.find((item: any) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  const enchantment = policyItem?.enchantment ?? 0;
  const reimbursement = enchantment >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD
    ? damage.amount * HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : damage.amount;
  return reimbursement - DEDUCTIBLE;
}
```
- Constants: `0` = 1 x 1 = **1**
- Bindings: `damage`, `policyItems`, `policyItem`, `item`, `enchantment`, `reimbursement` = 6 x 1 = **6**
- Invocations: `find()`, `===`, `??`, `>=`, `*`, `-` = 6 x 2 = **12**
- Conditionals: ternary = 1 x 4 = **4**
- Loops: 0 = **0**
- Assignments: 0 = **0**

**Subtotal for `calculateDamageReimbursement`: 23**

**New `calculateClaimResult` function:**
```
function calculateClaimResult(policy: any, incident: any): { payout: number; remainingCap: number } {
  const insuranceSum = calculateInsuranceSum(policy.items);
  const cap = insuranceSum * CAP_MULTIPLIER;
  const totalPayout = Math.floor(
    incident.damages.reduce(
      (sum: number, damage: any) => sum + calculateDamageReimbursement(damage, policy.items),
      0
    )
  );
  const remainingCap = cap - totalPayout;
  return { payout: totalPayout, remainingCap };
}
```
- Constants: `0` (reduce initial) = 1 x 1 = **1**
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `insuranceSum`, `cap`, `totalPayout`, `sum`, `damage`, `remainingCap` = 8 x 1 = **8**
- Invocations: `calculateInsuranceSum()`, `*` (cap), `Math.floor()`, `reduce()`, `+` (sum + ...), `calculateDamageReimbursement()`, `-` (cap - totalPayout) = 7 x 2 = **14**
- Conditionals: 0 = **0**
- Loops: `reduce` (iteration) = 1 x 5 = **5**
- Assignments: 0 = **0**

**Subtotal for `calculateClaimResult`: 28**

**Combined Total Mass: 23 + 28 = 51**

3. Net reduction of 11 mass

- Per-damage logic (policy item lookup, enchantment check, deductible subtraction) is isolated in its own function

- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Both functions now have a single clear responsibility
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Fewer assignments means less mutable state to track

- **Mass Change**: 62 -> 51 (change of -11)

content.text:
The code looks clean. Let me now document the complete refactoring analysis.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The key changes:

**Before** (in `calculateClaimResult` inline counting + `countByType`):
- `countByType` function: bindings(`items`, `counts`, `item`) = 3, loop = 5, assignment = 6, invocation(none extra) = 0, constant(`0`) = 1 = **15**
- Inline counting in `calculateClaimResult`: bindings(`damageCounts`, `damage`) = 2, loop = 5, assignment = 6, constant(`0`) = 1 = **14**
- Call to `countByType(policy.items)`: invocation = 2

Total for counting-related code: 15 + 14 + 2 = **31**

**After** (`countBy` with key extractor reused):
- `countBy` function: bindings(`items`, `keyFn`, `counts`, `item`, `key`) = 5, loop = 5, assignment = 6, invocation(`keyFn(item)`) = 2, constant(`0`) = 1 = **19**
- Call `countBy(incident.damages, (d) => d.itemType)`: invocation = 2, binding(`d`) = 1 = **3**
- Call `countBy(policy.items, (item) => item.type)`: invocation = 2, binding(`item`) = 1 = **3**

Total for counting-related code: 19 + 3 + 3 = **25**

**Mass Change for refactored section**: 31 --> 25 (delta: -6)

**Overall estimated Total Mass**: 295 --> 289 (delta: -6)

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Naming: Renamed `countByType` to `countBy` -- the function is now more general-purpose, and the name `countBy` better reflects that it counts by an arbitrary key function rather than being hardcoded to the `type` property.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Eliminated duplicated counting logic. The inline `for` loop in `calculateClaimResult` that counted damages by `itemType` was functionally identical to `countByType` but accessed a different property. By generalizing `countBy` to accept a key extractor function, both call sites now reuse the same function.
- Mass: Reduced from approximately 295 to 289 (delta: -6). Eliminated one loop, one assignment, and several bindings by removing the inline counting code.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): The refactored `calculateClaimResult` is now more readable. Instead of an inline loop that the reader must parse to understand what it does, there is a single call to `countBy` that clearly communicates "count damages by item type." The function body now starts with two clean declarative lines that set up the counts, followed immediately by the validation logic.

### Step 7: Report Completion

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Generalized `countByType` into `countBy` with a key extractor parameter, eliminating duplicated counting logic in `calculateClaimResult`.
- **Mass Change**: 295 --> 289 (delta: -6)
- **Tests**: All 40 passing.

content.text:
### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The refactoring added type interfaces which are structural declarations, not executable code. The interfaces themselves add:
- 9 new interface declarations with field definitions

However, the key change in the executable code is the **removal of inline type annotations** (`any` replaced by named types, redundant `: number` and `: any` annotations in lambda parameters removed). Let me recalculate the mass of the executable code.

The executable logic mass is essentially unchanged -- the same constants, bindings, invocations, conditionals, loops, and assignments exist. The interfaces add structure but not executable mass. The `countBy` function gained a generic `<T>` which is a type-level construct, not runtime mass.

**Mass Change**: ~337 (before) -> ~337 (after) -- mass is unchanged in executable code. The improvement is purely in Rule 2 (Reveals Intent).

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Replaced all `any` types with proper TypeScript interfaces (`Item`, `Customer`, `Damage`, `Incident`, `Policy`, `QuoteStep`, `ClaimStep`, `Scenario`, `QuoteResult`, `ClaimResult`)
- Made `countBy` generic with `<T>` instead of `any`
- Made the return type of `processScenario` explicit: `{ results: (QuoteResult | ClaimResult)[] }` instead of `unknown`
- Removed redundant inline type annotations in lambda parameters (e.g., `(sum: number, item: any)` became `(sum, item)` where types are inferred)

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Significantly improved. The domain model is now self-documenting. A reader can immediately understand what an `Item`, `Policy`, `Damage`, `Incident`, or `Scenario` contains without reading through the function bodies. The discriminated union `QuoteStep | ClaimStep` makes the step structure explicit. The return type `{ results: (QuoteResult | ClaimResult)[] }` tells callers exactly what to expect.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication was found to address.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: The interfaces add new type declarations, but these are justified by the substantial clarity gain (Rule 2 takes priority over Rule 4).

**Mass Change**: 337 -> 337 (unchanged in executable code; interfaces are type-level only)

### Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Replaced all `any` types with 10 named TypeScript interfaces; made `countBy` generic; made return types explicit
- **Mass Change**: 337 -> 337 (type-level improvement, no runtime mass change)
- **Tests**: All 41 passing

**Relevant files**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_15-29-15_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.1-testlist-scope-fix_opus-4-6-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` -- implementation with new type interfaces

This was the final test. All 41 tests are implemented and passing. The codebase is now type-safe with clear domain modeling.

content.text:
The MHPCO Claim Office kata is complete. Here's a summary:

**41 tests passing** covering:
- **Tests 1-7**: Base premiums for all item types (sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone)
- **Tests 8-13**: Building block discount (3 alike components = 60 G) and mixed component handling
- **Tests 14-22**: Premium modifiers (cursed surcharge, enchantment surcharge, loyalty discount, follow-up discount, and full integration)
- **Tests 23-37**: Claim processing (standard reimbursement, per-item deductible, enchantment >= 8 at 50%, dragon material full reimbursement, payout rounding, multiple same-type items, cap calculation, cap exhaustion across successive claims)
- **Tests 38-41**: Error cases (unknown item type, item not in policy, excess damages, negative damage amount)

**Files created:**
- `src/claim-office.ts` — Core implementation with typed interfaces and all business logic
- `src/claim-office.spec.ts` — 41 test cases
- `src/cli.ts` — CLI entry point reading JSON from stdin, writing to stdout
- `experiment-done.txt` — Done marker
